era_context: Early 16th century: the High Renaissance in Italy; the Portuguese maritime empire expanding under King Manuel I; the Ottoman Empire consolidating power; the Ming Dynasty in China under the Hongwu Emperor's successor; the Safavid Empire in Persia under Ismail I; the Spanish Reconquista completed, with Spain consolidating colonial holdings in the Americas and North Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal contacts beginning)
Interior of the Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary; Spanish conquest narratives dominate)

events.title: Michelangelo's David unveiled in Florence; completion of the marble statue
events.description: Michelangelo Buonarroti completed his monumental marble sculpture of David in early 1504 and unveiled it on 8 September 1504 in the Piazza della Signoria in Florence. The 17-foot-tall statue, carved from a single block of Carrara marble, represents the biblical hero as an idealized male nude in classical contrapposto. The work is considered one of the greatest achievements of the Renaissance and a masterpiece of human anatomical representation. Its public installation made it a symbol of Florentine civic identity and republican virtue. The statue remained in the Piazza della Signoria until 1873, when it was moved indoors for preservation.
key_figures: The Opera del Duomo (Florence Cathedral workshop)

sources.name: Michelangelo's letters and contemporary Florentine records
Archaeological and art historical analysis of the statue

events.certainty_note: The completion and unveiling dates are well-documented in contemporary sources and confirmed by art historical analysis.

events.title: Death of Queen Isabella I of Castile; succession of Joanna the Mad
events.description: Queen Isabella I of Castile died on 26 November 1504 after a long illness. She had been a dominant figure in Spanish politics for over 50 years, presiding over the completion of the Reconquista (1492), the sponsorship of Columbus's voyages, and the consolidation of Spanish power. Her death marked a significant transition in Spanish governance. Her daughter, Joanna of Castile (known as 'Joanna the Mad' due to her mental illness), inherited the throne, but her husband, Philip the Handsome, and her father, King Ferdinand II of Aragon, competed for power. This succession crisis would shape Spanish politics for the next decade.
key_figures: King Ferdinand II of Aragon (her husband)

sources.name: Spanish royal chronicles and contemporary records

events.certainty_note: Isabella's death date is precisely documented in Spanish royal records.

events.title: Leonardo da Vinci completes The Last Supper; begins work on other projects
events.description: Leonardo da Vinci completed his monumental fresco The Last Supper in the refectory of the Convent of Santa Maria delle Grazie in Milan in 1498 (not 1504; see disconfirming evidence). However, in 1504, Leonardo was actively working on other projects in Florence, including the cartoon for the Battle of Anghiari and studies for various paintings and inventions. By 1504, Leonardo was at the height of his creative powers and was recognized as one of the greatest artists and thinkers of the Renaissance. His influence on contemporary and subsequent artists was profound.
events.certainty_note: Leonardo's activities in 1504 are documented in his notebooks and contemporary records. The Last Supper was completed in 1498, not 1504.

events.title: Columbus's fourth voyage ends; return to Spain
events.description: Christopher Columbus returned to Spain in November 1504 after his fourth and final voyage to the Americas (1502–1504). The voyage had explored the coasts of present-day Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Panama. Columbus was in poor health, having suffered from arthritis, ophthalmia, and other ailments. He had lost royal favor and would spend his remaining years petitioning the Spanish crown for recognition and financial compensation. The voyage marked the effective end of Columbus's exploration career and the beginning of systematic Spanish colonization under other leaders such as Nicolás de Ovando.
key_figures: Queen Isabella I of Castile (died 26 November 1504)

sources.name: Ferdinand Columbus, Life of the Admiral

events.certainty_note: Columbus's return to Spain in November 1504 is well-documented in multiple sources.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor's successor, the Yongle Emperor's line continues; Ming Dynasty stability
events.description: In 1504, the Ming Dynasty under the Hongwu Emperor (who died in 1398) and his successors was in a period of relative stability. The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) had died in 1424, and his successors had maintained the dynasty's power and cultural achievements. By 1504, the Ming Dynasty was in the reign of the Hongwu Emperor's great-grandson, the Zhengde Emperor (who would ascend the throne in 1505). The dynasty was characterized by strong centralized governance, extensive bureaucracy, and significant cultural and artistic achievements. However, the growing power of eunuchs in the imperial court was beginning to create factional tensions.
key_figures: Hongwu Emperor (founder of Ming Dynasty, died 1398)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's political situation in 1504 is well-documented in official records.

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Cannanore, India
events.description: Portuguese forces established a fortified trading post at Cannanore (present-day Kannur) in Kerala, India, in 1504. This was part of the broader Portuguese strategy to monopolize the spice trade in the Indian Ocean. Cannanore, like Cochin, was a major spice port, and its control gave the Portuguese significant leverage over regional trade networks. The establishment of these trading posts marked the beginning of sustained European military and economic dominance in the Indian Ocean, displacing Arab and Swahili merchant networks that had dominated for centuries.

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and contemporary dispatches

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Portuguese trading post at Cannanore is attested in Portuguese sources, though the exact date (1504 vs. 1505) is sometimes uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: 1. Leonardo da Vinci completed The Last Supper in 1498, not 1504. In 1504, Leonardo was working on other projects in Florence. 2. The Hongwu Emperor died in 1398, not 1504. By 1504, the Ming Dynasty was in the reign of the Hongwu Emperor's great-grandson, the Zhengde Emperor (who would ascend in 1505). 3. The exact date of the Portuguese establishment of trading posts in India (Cannanore, Cochin) is sometimes uncertain, with dates varying between 1504 and 1505 in different sources.
historiographic_note: The year 1504 is well-documented in European sources (Italian Renaissance, Portuguese maritime expansion, Spanish succession crisis) and Chinese sources (Ming court records). Ottoman and Safavid sources are less detailed for this specific year. Sub-Saharan African and interior American sources are minimal; Portuguese and Spanish colonial narratives dominate. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and cultural events in Europe, the Mediterranean, and Asia; economic and social history of non-elite populations is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Michelangelo's David and the foundation stone of St. Peter's Basilica represent the apex of High Renaissance artistic and architectural achievement.
Isabella's death in 1504 initiated a succession crisis that was resolved with Charles V's accession in 1516.
Columbus's return to Spain in 1504 marked the end of his exploration career and the beginning of his final years, ending with his death in 1506.
